content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA 
CHAIRMAN 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
The Honorable Barbara Alby 
Assembly, California Legislature 
State Capitol 
P.O. Box 842849 
Sacramento, CA 94249-0001 
Re: Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-97-195 
Dear Assembly Member Alby: 
April 21, 1997 
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). I Since your question is general in nature and does not name a 
specific individual whose duties are in question, we can only provide informal advice.2 
QUESTION 
Who is responsible for enforcement of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions in special 
districts? 
CONCLUSION 
The answer varies depending on the type of violation and the type of enforcement action 
brought against the alleged violator. The Commission may assess administrative penalties for a 
violation of the Act, including conflict-of-interest violations. The district attorney is the civil 
prosecutor for non-statewide agencies, such as special districts, but may authorize the 
Commission to bring a civil action. The Commission is the civil prosecutor with respect to the 
state and statewide agencies, except itself. The Attorney General is the civil prosecutor with 
respect to the Commission. The Attorney General and the district attorney of the county in 
which the violation occurs have concurrent powers and responsibilities to enforce all the 
criminal provisions of the Act. The Commission has concurrent jurisdiction in enforcing the 
criminal misdemeanor provisions ofthe Act. City clerks have specific enforcement duties for 
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice. 
(Regulation 18329.) 
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 


File No. 1-97-195 
Page 2 
reporting violations. Also, an agency may discipline a public official for a conflicts violation. 
Finally, private causes of action may be brought by any person residing in the jurisdiction in 
certain circumstances. 
ANALYSIS 
The enforcement provisions of the Act are located in Sections 83116,83116.5, 89520, 
89521,91000-91015. 
1. 
Administrative Actions 
Section 83116 provides: 
"When the Commission determines that there is probable cause for 
believing this title has been violated, it may hold a hearing to 
determine if such a violation has occurred. Notice shall be given 
and the hearing conducted in accordance with the Administrative 
Procedure Act 
*** 
When the Commission determines on the basis of the hearing that a 
violation has occurred, it shall issue an order which may require 
the violator to: 
(a) Cease and desist violation of this title; 
(b) File any reports, statements or other documents or information 
required by this title; 
(c) Pay a monetary penalty of up to five thousand dollars ($5,000) 
per violation to the General Fund of the state. 
When the Commission determines that no violation has occurred, it 
shall publish a declaration so stating." 
The Commission may impose administrative penalties for any violation of the Act, 
including any conflict-of-interest violation. 
2. 
Civil Actions 
Section 91001 provides, in pertinent part: 
"(b) The civil prosecutor is primarily responsible for enforcement 
ofthe civil penalties and remedies of this title. The civil 
prosecutor is the commission with respect to the state or any state 
agency, except itself. The Attorney General is the civil prosecutor 
with respect to the commission. The district attorneys are the civil 


File No. 1-97-195 
Page 3 
prosecutors with respect to any other agency. The civil prosecutor 
may bring any civil action under this title which could be brought 
by a voter or resident of the jurisdiction. Upon written 
authorization from a district attorney, the commission may bring 
any civil action under this title which could be brought by a voter 
or a resident of the jurisdiction. Under such circumstances, 
Section 91007 shall not apply to the commission." 
On the civil side, in accordance with existing laws, where a violation involves the state or 
a state agency, such as a statewide ballot measure, the Commission has advised that it shall be 
the civil prosecutor. (Davidson Advice Letter, No. 1-89-612.) The district attorney is the civil 
prosecutor for violations of the Act with respect to any other agency. (See Kail Advice Letter, 
No. 1-90-724 and Jackson Advice Letter, No. A-76-493. [The district attorney is the civil 
prosecutor for violations of the Act occurring in connection with a county election].) "Agency" 
is defined as any state agency or local government agency. (Section 82003.) "Special district" 
means an agency of the state established for the local performance of governmental or 
proprietary functions within limited boundaries. (Section 82048.5.) Since violations that occur 
with regard to special districts do not involve the state or a state agency, but rather "any other 
agency," the district attorney would be the primary civil prosecutor. However, the district 
attorney may authorize the Commission to bring the same action. 
3. 
Criminal Actions 
On the criminal side, the Attorney General is responsible for enforcing the criminal 
provisions of this title with respect to state agencies, lobbyists as defined by the Act and state 
elections. The district attorney of any county in which a violation occurs has concurrent powers 
and responsibilities with the Attorney General. (Section 91001(a).) Therefore, for special 
districts the district attorney and the Attorney General would have concurrent powers and 
responsibilities. 
In addition, Section 91000(d), as amended by Proposition 208, gives the Commission concurrent 
jurisdiction in enforcing the criminal misdemeanor provisions of the Act. 3 
4. 
Reporting Violations 
In addition, the Act provides specific enforcement duties for all filing officers regarding 
late filings of certain statements and reports. (Sections 910 13 and 91013.5.) Filing officers may 
impose fines for the late filing of statements or reports and bring a civil action to obtain a 
judgment in small claims, municipal, or superior court for the purpose of collecting any unpaid 
monetary penalties, fees, or civil penalties. (See also, In re Rundstrom (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 188; 
3 "In any case in which a district attorney could act as the civil or criminal prosecutor under the provisions 
of this title, the elected city attorney of any charter city may act as the civil or criminal prosecutor with respect to 
any violations of this title occurring within the city." (Section 91001.5.) 


File No. 1-97-195 
Page 4 
Mathews Advice Letter, No. 1-89-663; Bramhall Advice Letter, No. 1-90-263.) Such 
enforcement actions brought by filing officers are in addition to any other penalties which may 
be assessed by the Commission or others under the Act. 
5. 
Agency Discipline 
Section 91003.5 provides that any person who violates a provision of Chapter 7 
(Conflicts of Interest) is subject to discipline by his or her government agency, including 
dismissal, consistent with any applicable civil service or other personnel laws, regulations and 
procedures. 
6. 
Private Causes of Action 
The Act also allows for private causes of action. Section 91003 provides that any person 
residing in the jurisdiction may sue for injunctive reliefto enjoin violations or to compel 
compliance with the provisions of this title. Section 91004 provides that any person who 
intentionally or negligently violates any of the reporting requirements of this act shall be liable in 
a civil action brought by the civil prosecutor or by a person residing within the jurisdiction for 
an amount not more than the amount or value not properly reported. Section 91005 provides: 
"(a) Any person who makes or receives a contribution, gift or 
expenditure in violation of Section 84300, 84304, 86202, 86203, or 
86204 is liable in a civil action brought by the civil prosecutor or 
by a person residing within the jurisdiction for an amount up to 
five hundred dollars ($500) or three times the amount of the 
unlawful contribution, gift or expenditure, whichever is greater. 
(b) Any designated employee or public official specified in Section 
87200, other than an elected state officer, who realizes an 
economic benefit as a result of a violation of Section 87100 or of a 
disqualification provision ofa Conflict of Interest Code is liable in 
a civil action brought by the civil prosecutor or by a person 
residing within the jurisdiction for an amount up to three times the 
value of the benefit." (Emphasis added.) 
Please be advised that all the procedural rules of Chapter 11 (Enforcement) must be 
followed in any enforcement action. If you have a specific enforcement case for which you 
would like to know the procedure, please contact the Commission. 


File No. 1-97-195 
Page 5 
5660. 
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
SGC:MC:ak 
Sincerely, 
Steven G. Churchwell 
General Counsel 
By: Marte Castafios 
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
